text.title: Interface Analysis: Enterprise Landscape and Governance
text.summary: Portfolio-level integration landscape register, shadow-IT discovery, four-pass agentic workflow, governance rules, and anti-patterns.

section.title: Integration Landscape Register

rule.statement: Maintain the landscape register as a text file (Markdown table or CSV) in git; never in a spreadsheet or wiki — agents need to read and diff it, and the diff between versions is the impact analysis.
rule.rationale: A spreadsheet-based register ages within one quarter without automated updates; text in git enables automated feeds from CMDB and API gateway, and makes register changes auditable via pull request.

rule.statement: Assign criticality tiers (1-4) with explicit policy: tier-1 interfaces require contract tests, breaking-change review, and 24/7 monitoring; tier-4 needs only a register row. Never let the agent set tier-1 without explicit human sign-off.
rule.rationale: Criticality is a business judgment with operational and financial consequences; an agent setting tier-1 without authority creates monitoring obligations and review gates that the owning team never agreed to.

section.reference: Seeds register from CMDB export + gateway dump

rule.statement: Run a dedicated shadow-IT pass against email/Slack/Workato/Power Automate/Excel-macros in addition to the CMDB and API gateway; shadow integrations are the most common source of audit findings and security incidents.
rule.rationale: Agents will not find what is not in their inputs; CMDB + gateway only covers governed integrations. Shadow IT — Zapier recipes, manual SFTP scripts on someone's laptop — never makes the official diagram and causes every audit finding.

rule.statement: Flag any gateway traffic without a matching CMDB entry as a governance violation; flag any register entry with no gateway traffic in 90 days as a retirement candidate.
rule.rationale: Active-traffic reality checks keep the register honest; without them, the register accumulates phantom interfaces and misses real ones, making it useless for impact analysis.

rule.statement: Run enterprise interface analysis as four sequential passes: discovery (raw inventory from CMDB + gateway + iPaaS), classification (business capability, criticality, sensitivity, ownership), governance diff (against contract standard), human review before publication.
rule.rationale: A single-pass agent that discovers, classifies, and governs simultaneously produces outputs without human validation at each stage; errors discovered at publication require re-running all earlier passes.

example.note: Discovery prompt constrains the agent to inputs only, preserving unknown sensitivity rather than defaulting to "internal".
example.code: You are an enterprise BA producing an integration landscape register. Inputs: CMDB export <path>, API gateway access log <path>, iPaaS export <path>. For each unique source-target pair produce one row matching the register template. Do NOT invent interfaces not present in inputs. Mark sensitivity as "unknown" if no data-classification tag is found. Output: register rows in Markdown table, plus suspected shadow integrations (signals: high traffic, no CMDB entry).

example.note: Governance diff prompt requires standard line number for every finding.
example.code: You are governing API contracts. Diff the register at <path> against the enterprise standard at <standards.md>. For each non-compliance return: IF-id, rule violated, severity (block/warn/info), minimal remediation. Cite the standard line number for every finding.

antipattern.description: Agent conflates "interface" with "endpoint" — one IF-ID becomes one URL path.
antipattern.reason: An interface is a logical contract that may expose many endpoints; collapsing to endpoint granularity produces an unmaintainable register that duplicates governance effort.

antipattern.description: Agent defaults sensitivity to "internal" when data classification is unknown.
antipattern.reason: "Internal" is a positive classification; unknown data may be Restricted. Defaulting to the lowest sensitivity class creates compliance risk; always emit "unknown" for human classification.

antipattern.description: Channel misclassification: async/file/EDI/SWIFT/HL7 channels squashed into "REST".
antipattern.reason: Protocol family determines error handling, retry logic, security baseline, and SLA requirements; a misclassified protocol produces a specification that is technically wrong for the actual channel.

rule.statement: For SOC2, ISO 27001, and GDPR Art. 30, the landscape register must include PII classification per data field, data flow direction, and processing purpose; generate these from the data catalogue, not from agent inference.
rule.rationale: Field-level PII truth lives in the data catalogue (Purview/Collibra/Atlan); landscape-level inference is structurally lossy and cannot meet the specificity required by Art. 30 records of processing activities.
